    xxxxxxxxxx

    Extract trajectories from Set1ext for PPN single-zone processing¶

    This notebook shows how to extract notebooks from Set1ext models, anywhere. These methods should be used to create trajectories for the OZoNe project

    FH (170621)

    This notebook lives in this repo: https://github.com/NuGrid/wendi-examples

    How ipython notebooks work¶

    If you have never used an ipython notebook, then here are the few basic rules you need to know:

    • each cell is a block of code or comments (in markdown, see pull-down menu above)
    • in each cell you can edit code, several lines using the Return key to create a newline
    • once you are happy with a block of code use the play button above, or just hit Shift-Return to execute the block of code
    • in this notebook you use the NuGridPy tools to analyze data, latest version on Github 
    xxxxxxxxxx

    Additional step: run the trajectory somewhere¶

    Then, this notebook is running the trajectory and look at some data.

    MP (290621)

    xxxxxxxxxx

    Initialize session¶

    [1]:
    xxxxxxxxxx
     
    %pylab ipympl
    from nugridpy import nugridse as mp
    from nugridpy import mesa as ms
    import os
    ​
    # default data location (try mirror if default
    # is not available)
    data_dir="/data/nugrid"    
    ​
    # mirror data locations (some mirrors 
    # may not be available at some time)
    ​
    # data_dir="/data/nugrid_cedar"  # may be default    
    # data_dir="/data/nugrid_rpod4"    
    # data_dir="/data/nugrid_vos"    
    ​
    ​
    ms.set_nugrid_path(data_dir)
    mp.set_nugrid_path(data_dir)
    Populating the interactive namespace from numpy and matplotlib

    xxxxxxxxxx

    Select the model you would like to etract a trajectory from¶

    Create MESA instance, plot Kippenhahn diagrams and select location of the trajectory to be extracted.

    [2]:
     
    mass_ = 20
    z_    = 0.02
    s=ms.star_log(mass=mass_,Z=z_)
    nugrid_path = /data/nugrid
closest set is set1.2 (Z = 0.02)
closest mass is 20.0

 reading ...100%

    ifig=107;close(ifig);figure(ifig)
    s.kip_cont(ifig=ifig)

    plotting patches

    Figure
    [4]:
     
    ifig=67;close(ifig);figure(ifig)
    s.tcrhoc()
    plt.gcf().subplots_adjust(left=0.25)    
    plt.gcf().subplots_adjust(bottom=0.25)    
    ​
    plt.savefig('testtcrhoc.pdf')
    Figure 
    xxxxxxxxxx

    We extract the thermodynamic trajectory from the mppnp output se files:

    The NuGrid post-processing model¶

    Initialise:

    [3]:
     
    pt=mp.se(mass=mass_,Z=z_, type='ppd_exp')
    nugrid_path = /data/nugrid
closest set is set1.2 (Z = 0.02)
closest mass is 20.0
Searching files, please wait.......
Reading preprocessor files
File search complete.
    [5]:
     
    cyc_exp = pt.se.cycles[len(pt.se.cycles)-1]
    cyc_exp_0 = pt.se.cycles[0]
    ​
    ​
    masscut =  max(pt.se.get(cyc_exp,'mass'))
    mass_coo = pt.se.get(cyc_exp,'mass')
    temp = pt.se.get(cyc_exp_0,'temperature')
    ​
    mass_coo_min = len(temp)
    j=0
    for i in temp:
        if i > 1.05e-9:
            dum = mass_coo[j]
            masscut = min(dum,masscut)
            mass_coo_min = min(j,mass_coo_min)
        j=j+1
    print(masscut)
     reading ['temperature']...100%2.736601987234811
    [7]:
     
    #pt.se.get(cyc_exp,'iso_massf','He-4')
    #test = pt.se.isomeric_states
    #ind_ = np.where(np.array(test) != 1)[0]
    ​
    #[pt.se.isotopes[i] for i in ind_]
    [6]:
    xxxxxxxxxx
     
    # getting mass and abundances for decayed isotope or element
    ​
    cyc_ = cyc_exp #cyc_exp_0
    ​
    mass = pt.se.get(cyc_,'mass')
    ​
    iso_list = [['Cd-106']]
    ​
    el_label = ['Cd-106']
    el_abund = []
    for i in iso_list:
        iso_abund = 0.; tmp = [pt.se.get(cyc_,'iso_massf',ii) for ii in i]; iso_abund = [np.sum(tmp,axis = 0)][0]
        el_abund.append(iso_abund)
        
    el_abund = np.array(el_abund)
    ​
     reading ['iso_massf']...100%
    [8]:
    x
    # making the plot for elements vs mass coordinates now
    %pylab ipympl
    ifig=616; plt.figure(ifig)
    ​
    symb = ['k-','m-','r-','b-','c-','g-','k--','m--','r--','b--','c--','k:','m--','r--','b--','c--']*5
    ​
    # index to skip what is not ejected
    ind_tmp = mass_coo_min
    ​
    ​
    mass_peak_iso ={}
    ​
    for i in range(len(el_abund)):
        plt.semilogy(mass[ind_tmp:],el_abund[i][ind_tmp:],symb[i],lw=2,label=el_label[i])
        mass_use = mass[ind_tmp:] ##considerer only M > m_cut
        X_use = el_abund[i][ind_tmp:] ## i -> isotope , ind_tmp -> m_cut
        imax = X_use.argmax()
        m_peak = mass_use[imax]
        X_peak = X_use[imax] 
        mass_peak_iso[el_label[i]] = (m_peak, X_peak)
    ​
    ​
    print (m_peak, X_peak)
    ​
    plt.legend(loc='lower right')
    plt.xlim(masscut,4.5); plt.ylim(1e-12,1.5)
    plt.xlabel('Mass coordinate Msun',fontsize=15); plt.ylabel('X',fontsize=15)
    plt.gcf().subplots_adjust(left=0.25)    
    ​
    #plt.axvline(x=1.844,c='k') # help you to find the manually the location of the peak
    ​
    ​
    ​
    plt.savefig('test_'+str(mass_)+'_Z'+str(z_)+'_isotope_ref.pdf')
    plt.show()
    Populating the interactive namespace from numpy and matplotlib
3.002716232398212 2.4706708827685167e-08
    Figure 
    xxxxxxxxxx
     
    ## Two ways of writing the trajectory
    ​
    ### nugridse method "trajectory" 
    This method writes a trajectory at constant mass. The docstring says it interpolates to the given mass coordinate. The output format does not have any headers and creates a blank file, which is not good.
    ​
    ### this function is super slow. Definitely it needs to be improved...
    [9]:
     
    m_coor = m_peak
    model_start = int(cyc_exp_0)
    model_stop = int(cyc_exp)
    sparsity_step = 5 # this is to not print all the steps... slow function, this should not be done.
    r,rho,temperature, time = pt.trajectory(model_start, model_stop,\
                                            sparsity_step,m_coor, age_in_sec=False, online=False)
     reading ['age']...96%96%..96%
    [10]:
    xxxxxxxxxx
     
    # format used for time coordinate in write_trajectory is stupid, it should be ES
    # I write the trajectory in this cell
    ​
    a = open('trajectory_'+str(m_coor)+'.dat', 'w')
    a.write('# time T rho \n')
    a.write('# YRS/SEC; T8K/T9K; CGS/LOG \n')
    a.write("# FORMAT: '(10x,A3)' \n")
    a.write('AGEUNIT = SEC \n')
    a.write('TUNIT   = T9K \n')
    a.write('RHOUNIT = CGS \n')
    a.write('ID = 0 \n')
    ​
    fmt_1 = '%E'
    for i in range(len(time)):
        a.write(str(fmt_1 % time[i]) + '   '+str(fmt_1 % temperature[i]) + '   '+str(fmt_1 % rho[i]) + ' \n')
        
    a.close()
    [12]:
     
    #%ls -lh traj_0.15.dat
    ​
    xxxxxxxxxx

    ascii_table method¶

    The ascii_table.writeTraj method of the ascii_table module of NuGridPy is meant to produce a standard trajectory format with header.

    Task A1: Use ascii_table.writeTraj in nugridse.trajectory for writing the actual trajectory.

    Task A2: Make sure that ascii_table.writeTraj trajectory is a valid nsource option in ppn.

    [13]:
     
    #from nugridpy import ascii_table as at
    [14]:
    xxxxxxxxxx
     
    #%rm trajectory.input
    #at.writeTraj(filename='trajectory.input', data=[time-time[0],temperature,rho], \
    #             ageunit=1, tunit=1, rhounit=0, idNum=0)
    [15]:
    xxxxxxxxxx
     
    #at.writeTraj?
    xxxxxxxxxx

    Extract initial abundance at beginning of trajectory¶

    This method produces an initial abundance file in some odd format, it does have a header, however, we have maybe other formats for this, such as the iniab.dat format?

    [11]:
    xxxxxxxxxx
     
    pt.abund_at_masscoordinate(model_start,m_coor,online=False)
     reading ['rho']...100%]...100%iso_massf
 reading ['iso_massf']...100%
